content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS, each character of which is either N\verb+N+N or P\verb+P+P. In one move, you can perform the following operation:


Pick an index 1≤i≤∣S∣1 \leq i \leq |S|1≤i≤∣S∣ and flip SiS_iSi​, i.e, if Si=NS_i = \verb+N+Si​=N set it to P\verb+P+P and vice versa.
Find the minimum number of moves required to obtain a string such that there exists a way to split it into several continuous substrings, each of which is either P\verb+P+P or NP\verb+NP+NP, and the number of P\verb+P+P substrings in this splitting equals the number of NP\verb+NP+NP substrings.

For example, NPP\verb+NPP+NPP and PNPPNP\verb+PNPPNP+PNPPNP can be split this way (into NP\verb+NP+NP + P\verb+P+P and P\verb+P+P + NP\verb+NP+NP + P\verb+P+P + NP\verb+NP+NP respectively) but NNPP\verb+NNPP+NNPP cannot.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of input of each test case contains the string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one integer — the minimum number of moves required to transform the string into the desired form.
constraints: SSS contains only characters N\verb+N+N and P\verb+P+P
Sum of ∣S∣|S|∣S∣ over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: Flip the second and third characters so that the string becomes NPP\verb+NPP+NPP, which can be broken into substrings NP\verb+NP+NP + P\verb+P+P. Here the count of substrings of both types is 111.
Test Case 222: The original string can already be split into P\verb+P+P + NP\verb+NP+NP, so no more moves are required.
Test Case 333: Flip the first and eighth characters so that the string becomes PPNPNPNPP\verb+PPNPNPNPP+PPNPNPNPP, which can be broken into substrings P\verb+P+P + P\verb+P+P + NP\verb+NP+NP + NP\verb+NP+NP + NP\verb+NP+NP + P\verb+P+P. Here the count of substrings of both types is 333.
